It is a strong option only if his AML single-cell analysis centers on immunology (immune cell populations, anti-leukemic immunity); a purely non-immune computational project would fall outside the immunology remit.

cur-career-development-program-cdp-speci.why: The Special Fellow tier targets more advanced postdocs (roughly 3-5 years) needing extra mentored time before independence, and like the Fellow tier it is open worldwide to non-US citizens with hematologic-malignancy-relevant research, so his AML ML work qualifies. At ~2 years post-PhD Jun is slightly early for this tier and would more naturally fit the Fellow level now, making this a strong option a year or two later. Same requirement for an AML/hematology-anchored project applies.

jhu-postdoctoral-fellowship-hope-funds-f.why: Hope Funds supports innovative postdoctoral work on difficult-to-treat cancers with no citizenship requirement provided the research is at a US nonprofit institution, and AML fits its blood-cancer/oncology scope. Jun's early-postdoc timing works because the 2-year-in-sponsor's-lab rule applies only if already appointed. The competition emphasizes highly innovative science, so a distinctive ML methods angle on AML would strengthen the case.

cur-ruth-l-kirschstein-nrsa-individual-p.why: The F32 supports mentored postdoctoral training and NCI funds computational/quantitative cancer projects, so an AML ML project with a defined cancer focus is fundable and it pairs well as a stepping stone toward a K99/R00. The decisive caveat is the statutory NRSA requirement of US citizenship, national status, or permanent residency at time of award; Jun is currently ineligible but could qualify if his PR (expected ~2027) is in hand before an award. This makes it a plausible future rather than immediate option.

cur-mentored-research-scientist-developm.why: The K01 offers protected mentored time for PhD scientists building an independent program and many institutes use it for computational skill development, so it suits Jun's oncology pivot, though the K25 is better tailored when prior biomedical experience is limited. Like other NIH career awards it requires US citizenship or permanent residency at time of award, so eligibility depends on his PR coming through (~2027) before an award is made. It is best viewed as a contingent, later-stage option.

jhu-proposals-in-leukemia-research-when-.why: This RFA solicits laboratory, translational, or clinical research on acute leukemia and is open to all career stages with no stated citizenship requirement, making it one of the most on-topic AML-specific options for Jun's disease focus. The practical caveat is that it is oriented toward investigators running their own projects, so a postdoc would typically apply through or alongside a mentor/PI. The one-year renewable structure suits a focused computational AML aim.

cur-leukemia-exploration-and-prevention-.why: LEAP is a blood-cancer RFA centered on RUNX1-FPD and associated hematologic malignancies, particularly AML, and it explicitly welcomes computational and systems approaches, aligning tightly with Jun's AML methods. The major caveat is eligibility: it targets full-time independent investigators, so as a postdoc without an independent faculty appointment he is likely not yet eligible and would revisit this after transition. The scientific scope is also narrow (RUNX1 predisposition) relative to general AML.

jhu-clra-research-grants-children-s-leuk.why: This recurring grant funds research into the causes and cures of leukemia with no explicit career-stage restriction, so it is thematically aligned with Jun's AML focus and could support a computational aim. Research must be US-based, which fits his [redacted]-area setting, and no citizenship requirement is stated. The modest award size and typical orientation toward a supervising investigator mean a postdoc would likely apply with mentor support.

cur-lrf-postdoctoral-fellowship-grant-ly.why: This lymphoma fellowship is methodology-agnostic and open to PhD-trained (non-MD) postdocs within 5 years of the degree, with a US/Canada work-visa pathway that fits Jun's status, so a computational classifier or outcome-prediction project would qualify. The caveat is disease fit: it funds lymphoma rather than AML, so he would need to reorient onto a lymphoid malignancy to be competitive. It is a good adjacent option if his methods generalize beyond myeloid disease.

cur-acs-flatiron-health-real-world-data-.why: This ACS+Flatiron award is explicitly data-science-focused, leveraging real-world oncology data, so it fits an AI/ML researcher and could accommodate hematologic-malignancy questions if Jun works with EHR-derived datasets. Two caveats temper the fit: his core expertise is single-cell RNA-seq rather than real-world clinical data, and applicants are generally expected to already be within the ACS research ecosystem, which may gate a first-time applicant. It is a stretch option contingent on a real-world-data angle.

cur-mmrf-research-fellowship-program-mul.why: This one-year MMRF fellowship supports postdocs doing mentored myeloma research and is a lower-barrier entry point where a computational project would fit its data-rich (e.g., CoMMpass) environment. The principal caveat is disease mismatch: it funds multiple myeloma, not AML, so Jun would need to redirect his single-cell modeling onto myeloma to be eligible in spirit. Best considered only if he is open to a plasma-cell-malignancy application of his methods.

jhu-damon-runyon-fellowship-award-damon-.why: This prestigious postdoctoral fellowship funds basic and physician-scientists in cancer research with no citizenship requirement (research at a US institution), and computational cancer projects are eligible, so the science fits Jun's AML work. The binding caveat is timing: the basic-scientist track expects the PhD within roughly 18 months of the deadline and under one year in the sponsor's lab, and as a 2nd-year postdoc who earned his PhD in 2024 he is likely past that window. The more open-ended Damon Runyon Quantitative Biology Fellowship is the better-matched sibling program for him.

cur-rally-foundation-research-grants-pos.why: Rally offers a postdoctoral-fellow grant tier and explicitly encourages data-utilization and innovative approaches, so an ML/computational project would be welcomed and Jun's PhD-postdoc stage is eligible. The key constraint is that research must target childhood/pediatric cancer, so this fits only if his AML work addresses pediatric AML rather than adult disease. It is a viable option if he can frame a pediatric-leukemia application of his single-cell methods.

cur-mmrf-research-scholars-program-schol.why: This flagship MMRF award funds basic/translational myeloma research and its eligibility caps applicants at assistant-professor level, which technically admits a postdoc, and its multi-omic CoMMpass data is well suited to ML modeling. Two caveats limit fit: it is primarily an early-independent-investigator award more naturally held by junior faculty, and it funds myeloma rather than Jun's AML focus. He would need both a myeloma reorientation and a strong independence case to compete.

cur-ruth-l-kirschstein-nrsa-institutiona.why: A T32 slot in a computational-biology, cancer, or hematology training program would provide NRSA stipend support and structured training aligned with Jun's AML methods, and [redacted]/Harvard/Broad host relevant programs he could seek appointment to. The decisive caveat is the statutory NRSA citizenship/permanent-residency requirement at appointment, so he is not currently eligible and could only be appointed once his PR (expected ~2027) is granted. It is an institutional appointment rather than something he applies for directly to NIH.

cur-jane-coffin-childs-postdoctoral-fell.why: The Fund's cancer/medical-research mission and openness to computational projects and non-US citizens at US institutions make the science and status a fit for Jun's blood-cancer ML work. The blocking caveat is the early-career window: it requires the PhD within about 24 months and no more than 12 months of prior postdoc experience, and as a 2nd-year postdoc he has likely aged out of eligibility. It would only be viable if his exact PhD date and lab-start timing still fall inside those limits.

cur-helen-hay-whitney-foundation-postdoc.why: This foundation funds all basic biomedical science including computational blood-cancer research and allows foreign citizens to hold it in US labs, so the disease and citizenship dimensions fit Jun. The limiting caveat is that it is designed for very early postdocs, requiring the PhD within roughly two years and no more than one year in the postdoc lab, and a 2nd-year postdoc almost certainly exceeds the one-year-in-lab limit. It is effectively a missed-window option unless his timing is unusually recent.
